summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that the workflow import endpoint at GET /workflows/from-url is vulnerable to SSRF. An authenticated attacker can use this endpoint to access internal network resources and cloud metadata endpoints that should not be externally accessible.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should demonstrate that an authenticated user can access internal metadata endpoints via the SSRF vulnerability, retrieving sensitive information such as AWS IAM credentials or internal service data that should be protected by network boundaries.
actual_observed_result: The reproduction successfully started n8n, created an authenticated user session, and exploited the SSRF vulnerability to access a mock internal metadata endpoint. The endpoint accepted the arbitrary URL without validation and returned the internal metadata content, confirming unauthorized internal network access.
verification_method: The verification script orchestrates a complete SSRF attack: (1) builds and starts n8n, (2) creates authenticated user session, (3) starts a mock internal metadata server, (4) exploits the SSRF endpoint with a URL pointing to the internal service, (5) verifies that internal metadata is returned in the response. Success is confirmed when the response contains the expected metadata fields (aws_metadata, secret_key) that should not be accessible via external API endpoints.
evidence_produced_by_run: n8n server started successfully and health check passed
User authentication completed and API token obtained
SSRF payload delivered via GET /rest/workflows/from-url endpoint
Internal metadata endpoint accessed and data returned in response
No SSRF protections (IP filtering, domain allowlist, protocol restrictions) were enforced

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the n8n repository from GitHub on the host, checks out the v2.4.0 tag, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
